data.title: FSCO: Big Downside Risk And Almost Certain Yield Cut (Downgrade)
data.description:
In July this year, I published my first article on FS Credit Opportunities Fund (NYSE:FSCO). The thesis was bullish and based on conservative leverage, a high-quality portfolio, and a very competitive yield (again, despite the immaterial debt component). And by competitive, I mean FSCO's yield versus a typical level that we can lock in from BDC (BIZD) investments. At the moment when I issued the previous article, FSCO offered a yield of ~11.3%, which was almost exactly in line with the BDC sector average. You can find more details in the article about the reasons why FSCO is actually quite similar to BDCs, but the main ones are: 1) exposure to private credit loans, 2) mostly senior secured first-lien loans, 3) relatively modest exposure to equity-like products, and 4) presence of external leverage, which is important to magnify the positive spread effect from high-yielding investments and less expensive cost of capital.

What has happened since the release of my previous article is that both FSCO and BDCs have experienced a notable volatility.

Yet, the difference between BDC and FSCO performance is just massive.

On the one hand, we can now buy FSCO at a slightly better price level and still access ~11.5% yield. For example, FSCO trades at a discount of 1.8%, which is quite good given that the 52-week high was at 3.3%.

On the other hand, the BDC space, due to the recent pain, is now trading at a bargain basement level. For example, the sector median P/NAV is 0.81x - i.e., a discount of 19%. And here I am not talking about a sector median, which is constituted of speculative names. Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (NYSE:MSDL) is a great example. It is a high-quality BDC with below-sector-average leverage, top-tier earnings quality (i.e., immaterial PIK), 90%+ of portfolio consisting of non-cyclical names, etc., and still the P/NAV is at 0.80x.

Given the above, my logical question is whether FSCO still is a buy. And I am looking at this from two angles: 1) FSCO as a stand-alone and yield-driven case, and 2) FSCO as a relative investment case in the context of very similar alternatives (i.e., BDCs).

Thesis review

Let's start with negatives first.

The First Brands bankruptcy has indeed sent shock waves across the private credit industry, which, apart from the interest rate factor, has, in my view, been one of the key reasons for the discounted pricing.

FSCO is among those private credit vehicles that have suffered from this particular event:

Luckily, FSCO's exposure to First Brands has been limited—less than 2% of the total asset base. But if we strip out First Brands from FSCO's loan book completely, then basically we arrive at price-to-NAV alignment.

So, I think that it would be fair to say that the First Brand aspect does not move the needle in terms of making a decision whether to invest or keep capital in FSCO.

However, the next topic is more critical, to say the least.

Based on the most recent financials, FSCO's dependency on PIK (payment-in-kind) proceeds is just massive. PIK accounts for almost 20% of the total income generation.

The situation gets worse if we measure PIK against what matters more - i.e., net investment income. The PIK/NII ratio is about 31%, which basically means that one-third of the income that FSCO receives comes in the form of a non-cash item or a promise that upon loan maturity FSCO will be able to convert all of these PIK proceeds into cash.

This is, obviously, a high-risk assumption, especially in the current market environment, where the economy is not in its best shape.

Speaking of interest rate risk, FSCO is exposed to the same problem as most BDCs. Namely, the lion's share of its asset base is linked to floating rates, which move in tandem with the base rates that the Fed has already cut by 25 bps this year and is likely to cut even more in the near-term.

It is more than clear that the direction of travel in the NII per share generation is unfavorable, and already in Q3, 2025, we should observe some decline (mostly interest rate driven, although we don't know what the net effects will be from the FV adjustments).

Now, the thing is that FSCO's yield of ~11.5% is already insufficiently covered by cash flows based on Q2, 2025 results. The NII per share (H1 basis) $0.36, while the distribution per share is $0.39.

In my view, a dividend cut here is simply inevitable.

Having said that, there is one huge positive that we have to appreciate. It is the debt side of the equation. Namely, as of Q2, 2025, FSCO had a net debt to equity of 0.30x, which is much lower than the BDC sector average of 1.19x. Even if we include the preferred shares, the debt to equity figure for FSCO looks very strong, at 0.47x.

So, I would say that the slight underfunding of the base dividend that I highlighted above is not only logical, but actually indicative of relatively solid situation. Think of it. The average BDC is leveraged at 1.19x, and the relevant BDC base dividend coverage level is 101%. With more than twice as high leverage, BDCs are operating with almost identical dividend coverage levels.

The bottom line

Relative to BDCs, FSCO's position is attractive. FSCO carries much lower debt and thus lower financial risk and/or stronger income growth potential that could come in handy in offsetting the forthcoming pressures from lower base rates and loan write-downs. At the same time, the difference between yield coverage with NII generation is not significant, certainly immaterial in the context of a way more equitized balance sheet.

At the same time, we should not ignore the PIK component, which has become a more significant risk zone considering the weakening economy and potentially more private credit-linked bankruptcies that might follow after First Brand. The fact that almost one-third of NII per share stems from PIK sounds scary now, when the general preference should be towards high margin of safety vehicles.

With this in mind, I have to downgrade FSCO and liquidate my position before it is too late. In my previous article - FSCO: Double-Digit Yield And Alpha Compared To BDCs, It's A Buy - I saw a clear alpha potential relative to BDCs. Looking at the very first chart in the article, we can see that the thesis has played out. Yet, my base case now is that we will in the near term see a similar correction in FSCO as most BDCs have experienced over the past couple of weeks, month.

data.title: SQQQ: A Strategy For A Nasdaq At Its Highs
data.description:
Valuations are at pre-dot.com bubble highs, and both Powell and Jamie Dimon, and the BOE, together with the IMF, are calling for declines, yet I still think that ProShares UltraPro Short QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:SQQQ) is not a suitable hedging tool for this situation.

Anyway, given the strong uncertainty due to the potential introduction of new tariffs against China and the new restrictions on rare earth exports, I’d like to share with you a short-term trading strategy I recently discussed with a colleague from the bank wealth area. I’ll tell you in advance that this hasn’t changed my opinion on using SQQQ as a portfolio hedge. But let’s go in order.

Small recap - Decay Effect risk

For those who haven’t read my first coverage:

SQQQ is a ProShares ETF (leader in the leveraged and inverse ETFs segment), which uses the Nasdaq 100 index as a benchmark, but with the goal of providing a -3x of its daily performance, and NOT on a cumulative basis. All this with a TER of 0.98%.

What’s the unpleasant consequence of this replication process? That if the exposure is rebalanced daily, the long-term return will not be precisely -3x that of the Nasdaq 100 index. It’s called the decay effect, and it’s the reason why, in my opinion, it absolutely cannot have a hedging function in a portfolio. In practical terms, a simple pullback after a drop can erase or turn into unrecoverable losses any gains obtained; exactly as happened during the V-shaped recovery of April 2025 (which I had warned about).

Therefore, in a growth market like the Nasdaq, which has been rising for over 80% of the time, the risk of losing capital from using SQQQ is extremely high, and the trade-off plays against those who buy it.

The comment of one of my colleagues

Today, however, the matter becomes dynamic: one of my colleague advisors is adding these instruments to some clients’ portfolios: “The market is stretched, and instead of monetizing profits on Nasdaq 100-themed ETFs, I prefer to take SQQQ (⅓ of the exposure held on Nasdaq 100), and if Nasdaq continues, I monetize, and at least he doesn’t gain anything but records a capital loss; if it goes down, he’s hedged, he hasn’t liquidated the Nasdaq 100, and simply there’s no capital gain recorded except for any profits from the SQQQ trade.”

Does this reasoning make sense?

On one thing he’s definitely right: today’s market is stretched.

These two indicators can help:

  • The forward P/E of the info tech sector alone (where the bubble should be concentrated) is above the 98th percentile of the 10-year distribution, and the 87th of the 30-year one. Even considering the strong expected EPS growth, over 20% in 2026 (in line with Q3 25 evidence), valuations would still remain high. This is further confirmed, more broadly and realistically, by the extremity of the Shiller CAPE ratio.

  • The Buffett indicator has exceeded 220%, corresponding to the second standard deviation above the mean of the distribution, signaling a strong (excessive) divergence between the real and financial economy.

How do I see it?

Let’s start right away from the assumption that an “expensive” market doesn’t mean a “valueless” market.

If we think about it, at the core there is a situation diametrically opposite to the dot-com bubble: AI companies’ profit margins are wide, which generates a positive effect on compounded earnings per share, supported by constant guidance increases and positive surprises.

And yet in these days there has been a potential negative catalyst: the U.S.–China trade war could worsen with China’s new restrictions on rare earths.

And President Trump’s response, threatening new trade measures, is worrying the markets. Without going too deep into the topic, it would simply have a direct impact on the supply chain of tech companies working with AI. Today we’ve seen very stretched valuations, but also strongly growing expected EPS. Well, it would be quite a blow if those EPS failed to meet expectations right now.

So, being aware that there could be a potential negative catalyst, how do I manage the strong decay risk in the portfolio? Because we know well that SQQQ is not a simple hedging instrument you can put in your portfolio and forget about.

Strategy

In reality, it’s simpler than it seems: a leveraged inverse hedge. It tries to create neutrality on the Nasdaq, with a position of about 1/3 in SQQQ, and then, to limit the decay problem it performs a sort of rolling hedge using technical anchors. Simply put, if today a potential negative catalyst appears (Trump tariffs), it means there’s potential selling pressure on the Nasdaq.

Potentially, the VIX should rise, and today it’s still at 16 points. Then, the RSI of SQQQ should easily surpass 50 points on the 1D timeframe, but today it’s still below. Friday’s spike in SQQQ also created a technical reference for the stop. And my colleague simply opens a hedge, which he will close if the uptrend on the VIX stops, and SQQQ returns to 30 points and/or breaks again below the low of the bullish “cycle.” In that case, the catalyst would indeed have been “false.”

Risk

There still remain concrete risks that could lead the reader to consider other markets instead. Simply put, treating it as a trading operation still doesn’t make it a hedge, as could more simply be the case with classic gold or other instruments I’ve analyzed, which I’ll link here.

At the same time, SQQQ amplifies movements by -3x even in the pre-market, which potentially exposes the investor to imbalances in the risk/reward ratio compared to the backtest, due to gap. The increase in market volatility does not play in favor of this kind of strategy, especially on leveraged ETFs.

Then, personal consideration: if you accept paying a premium for a hedge, why not go directly to the options market? But that’s another story, separate from SQQQ.

Conclusion

To conclude, even though the SQQQ management strategy described is interesting, it’s not a concrete method on SQQQ that works as a hedge without a real risk or an indirect cost. It’s rather a trading strategy. And simply, that’s what I think SQQQ was created for.

Today I switch the rating from SELL to HOLD only because I find SQQQ at least intriguing from a short-term speculative perspective, but I would never naturally include it as a portfolio holding.

data.title: SunOpta's Latest Results Show It's Still On Track
data.description:
The health-food manufacturer SunOpta Inc. (NASDAQ:STKL) is best known for producing plant-based beverages. These are plant-based milks like soy milk and oat milk, but dairies don’t like calling them milks, so it’s more diplomatic to call them beverages. The company is now expanding its healthy fruit snack production as well. While its products are frequently sold at health-food stores, those aren’t its only customers in 2025. Big-box stores like Walmart Inc. (WMT) have begun to stock more health food. Additionally, coffee shops like Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) are making beverages that use plant-based milks as ingredients.

SunOpta’s stock has experienced some large fluctuations over the past decade, but the long-term trend has been sideways. The company is operating in growing market niches, but its products could be considered commodities. There are several companies that make plant-based milk, including the almond company Blue Diamond (privately held) that makes Almond Breeze milk, and Oatly Group AB (OTLY). And SunOpta’s revenue has been rising while its margins have been falling. But now it appears that SunOpta has stabilized its margins and will be able to expand them further in 2026. So this might be a good time to buy this stock.

This is a small-cap food manufacturer that had a market cap of $695 million after the close on October 8, 2025. And again, it has a lot of big customers in the food industry, including grocery stores, restaurants, and club stores. So this company has a lot of growth potential, but it might not be a well-known company because of its size. The first thing I’d start with is explaining why investors should look into this stock.

SunOpta Is Gaining Market Share in Growing Markets

According to Grand View Research, the US plant-based milk market is expected to grow at a rate of 5.7% over the 2024-2030 period. The North American plant-based milk market is expected to grow at an even faster rate of 6.8% over this time frame. SunOpta is an international company that has production facilities in Canada, so it can benefit from the growth of the Canadian market as well. Its ticker is listed on the Toronto exchange as well as the Nasdaq. This company’s international operations also expose it to tariffs, which I’ll discuss later on.

SunOpta’s 2Q 2025 10-Q provides its revenue by division. Its beverages and broths division, which includes plant milks, reported that revenue grew 10.6% quarter-over-quarter to $149.149 million. This division also generated 77.9% of its revenue for the quarter, with the remainder provided by its fruit snacks division and its ingredients division. While the company doesn’t directly report its revenue from plant-based milks, the performance of its beverages and broths division implies that its plant-based milk sales are growing faster than the overall market.

The fruit-based snack market is also growing. According to Global Market Insights, the North American fruit-based snack market was worth $1.7 billion in 2024 and was expected to grow at a 3.5% CAGR over the next 10 years. SunOpta’s fruit snacks division is also growing at a faster rate than this market as a whole. According to the 10-Q, SunOpta’s fruit snacks revenue rose 27.5% quarter-over-quarter to $38.182 million in the second quarter.

SunOpta also has a small ingredients division. This division’s revenue fell from $4.770 million in the second quarter of 2024 to $4.158 million in the second quarter of 2025. Conditions have been challenging for restaurants recently, so they might have ordered fewer ingredients. But SunOpta still reported great overall results for the second quarter. Its total revenue rose 12.9% quarter-over-quarter to $191.5 million.

SunOpta’s Plans to Improve Its Margins

Over the last few years, SunOpta’s margins have fallen. Its gross margin was 16.2% in 2024, 17% in 2023, and 17.6% in 2022. And it appears that the company’s gross margin will still fall further in 2025, although this isn’t entirely its fault. During the second-quarter 2025 earnings call, CEO Brian Kocher said that the company’s adjusted gross margin was 15.2%, and this included a 90 basis point impact from tariffs. To justify an investment in this company, you’d have to explain how it would have higher margins in 2026.

Kocher also said that SunOpta convinced its customers to fully absorb the increase in tariff costs, starting in the fourth quarter. This is an impressive achievement because its customers include large big-box stores and grocery store chains. These firms can be hard to bargain with because lots of manufacturers want to sell their food there. Smaller manufacturers don’t have a lot of negotiating leverage. SunOpta is a relatively small company in comparison with a store like Walmart or Albertsons Companies, Inc. (ACI).

Additional margin improvements could come from more efficient use of the company’s manufacturing equipment. It explained this in its first-quarter 2025 presentation. SunOpta can make more aseptic packages, such as shelf-stable plant milk and packaged fruit snacks, with the equipment it has now. It doesn’t need to build additional capacity to grow its sales in 2025, and it can raise its margins by generating more sales in 2026 while fixed costs remain the same. But there’s also another factor to consider, the Midlothian plant.

In 2023, SunOpta opened a large manufacturing facility in Midlothian, Texas. The plant cost $125 million to build, and its North Texas location will make it convenient to deliver plant-based milk and creamers to stores around the country. But SunOpta hasn’t been able to install the wastewater processing equipment that the plant needs yet. During the first-quarter 2025 earnings call, Kocher explained that SunOpta is currently paying $500,000 per quarter to dispose of wastewater from the plant. The plant’s wastewater processing equipment should be in place by the middle of 2026, so it won’t need to pay the disposal fee anymore. SunOpta’s adjusted EBITDA guidance considers the temporary nature of some of the expenses it’s incurring at the Midlothian plant.

During the first-quarter earnings call, Kocher also forecast that SunOpta would achieve an 18-19% gross margin by the fourth quarter of 2025, and would maintain its gross margin at that level in 2026. During the second-quarter call, the CEO said that “we’re doing what we said we would do,” which implies that the company is maintaining the guidance provided in the first quarter.

High Depreciation Costs Affect SunOpta’s Valuation

The interesting thing here is that after the market close on October 8, SunOpta’s forward EBIT was 21, 57% higher than the sector average. But its forward EBITDA was 11, which was just 5% higher than the sector average. And SunOpta’s forward P/S ratio was 0.86x, which was 22% lower than the sector average.

It appears that SunOpta is reporting high depreciation costs on its new manufacturing plant in Texas, and this is making the company look less profitable than it would be if those expenses were lower. In the second-quarter 2025 10-Q, it reported depreciation and amortization costs of $9.96 million. Its net income for that quarter was $4.351 million.

Risks

The biggest risk factor for SunOpta is that tariffs or worsening trade relations between the US and Canada could create additional problems for the company. During the second-quarter earnings call, the company explained that it negotiated deals with suppliers that protected it from tariffs, but then was impacted by revised tariffs on August 1 that will now reduce its third-quarter margins. It sounds like its input costs aren’t hedged, so if tariff policies change again in 2026 this could affect its results. And Canada and the US have been negotiating trade policies in October. Other food manufacturers might not be as dependent on the US and Canada maintaining good relations.

Valuation for SunOpta

As the CEO explained during the earnings call, it appears that SunOpta is currently on track to achieve its projections. Using the midpoint of the guidance provided during the second-quarter earnings call, SunOpta expects revenue of $810 million and adjusted EBITDA of $110 million for the full year of 2025. And the company’s guidance for 9% revenue growth and 15% annual EBITDA growth was maintained as well.

If SunOpta achieved these targets, it would report $883 million in revenue and $126 million in EBITDA for the full year of 2026. Its current EV is $1.11 billion. That includes $695 billion in market cap, $405 million in debt, $2 million in cash, and $15 million in other.

With an EV/EBITDA of 11 in 2026, SunOpta would have an EV of $1.39 billion. Subtract $405 million in debt and $15 million in other from that figure and add $2 million in cash, and its market cap would be $972 million.

SunOpta also bought back shares during the second quarter, but its share count still rose by 700,000 to 117.3 million in the first half of 2025. And it rose 2.4 million in the full year before that. So I’ll be conservative and estimate that the company will have 120 million shares outstanding at the end of 2026. That results in a target price of $8.10 per share in 2026, which is 36% higher than the closing price of $5.97 per share on October 8.

SA analysts and the SA quant score currently rate SunOpta a buy and Wall Street rates it a strong buy.

Conclusion

SunOpta’s results for the second quarter show that its plan is currently working and it is likely to have higher margins by the end of 2026. The CEO is correct, the company did what it said it would do. Additionally, demand is rising for both plant-based milks and healthy fruit snacks. And SunOpta’s strong revenue growth indicates that it is capturing market share in both categories as well. Additionally, high depreciation costs make this company’s performance look worse than it really is. Investors are also confident in this company. Tariffs are a real risk factor, but this company has been able to get buyers to absorb tariff costs. So I say SunOpta is a strong buy.

data.title: Soleno: Blockbuster Launch In Progress
data.description:
This is my third Soleno Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SLNO) article, following 06/2025's "Soleno Therapeutics: Overbought On VYKAT Approval Enthusiasm". In Overbought, I rated Soleno as a "Hold". It has since dropped ~24%. I am taking another look to see how its future prospects are shaping up now that it has shaken off a bit of its initial approval froth.

Soleno is in show me mode with its hefty market cap and total reliance on VYKAT XR.

I have often referred to the daunting period in an emerging commercial stage biotech company's life between FDA approval and successful commercialization as the danger zone. This is where Soleno finds itself following its 03/2025 FDA approval of its VYKAT XR (diazoxide choline, DCCR) in treatment of hyperphagia in adults and children 4 years of age and older with Prader-Willi syndrome [PWS].

Its ability to successfully navigate these perilous shoals will depend to a significant degree on a variety of key factors, including its:

  1. financial strength;
  2. strength of its FDA label;
  3. competition;
  4. intellectual property protection;

I will address each of these factors below as they apply to Soleno as it initiates VYKAT's launch.

Financial strength

During Soleno's 08/07/2025, Q2/2025 earnings Call CFO MacKaness reported on its powerful liquidity position as it begins its launch of VYKAT XR. At 06/30/2025 quarter's end it had $293.8 million of cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities.

Subsequently it raised an additional $230 million through an underwritten offering of its common stock; this raised its total pro forma cash balance >$500 million. This liquidity seems overly fulsome when compared to its historic annual cash burn, as reflected by this table excerpt below showing Soleno's net cash used in operating activities for years 2022-2024:

According to MacKaness' response to a question during the Call as shown below, Soleno is expecting significantly increased expenditures as its launch progresses along with its efforts to expand VYKAT's approvals internationally:

...our cash OpEx, I think we've mentioned in the past, we think about $120 million to $130 million for this year. We may find that there's a reason to sort of increase that a little bit, but there wouldn't be much more than $140 million. And that would just be doing things, like I said, putting some early sort of initiatives in Europe, as we've mentioned.

There's also some life cycle management stuff we might fire up as well. So still very well managed for 2025. And then if we go into 2026, that will increment. And I think, again, we mentioned sort of it probably go north of $150 million,...

He then goes on to address the revenue side of things. CEO Bhatnagar had already pointed to VYKAT's $32.7 million in net revenues for the second quarter. While MacKaness suggested that this initial success could cool in successive quarters, he ultimately anticipates:

...getting to cash flow positive in -- somewhere in the near term.

The bottom line in terms of liquidity is that there is no reason to expect that Soleno will need additional cash raises for the foreseeable future.

Strength of its FDA label

The following shows the opening page of VYKAT XR's FDA label:

This label is quite simple and straightforward, with no black box warning and few warnings or precautions of any kind. In terms of indications and usage it is clear:

VYKAT XR is indicated for the treatment of hyperphagia in adults and pediatric patients 4 years of age and older with Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS).

Competition

Soleno's 10-K sets out its competitive matrix. It notes that there are only two FDA approved PWS related therapies, Genotropin (somatropin), and Omnitrope (somatropin) other than VYKAT; they are approved only for growth failure due to PWS. It also notes that there are sundry therapies in clinical development.

These include molecules from:

  1. Acadia Pharmaceuticals (ACAD) — in 09/2025 Acadia announced that its phase 3 PWS candidate failed to show statistically significant improvement over placebo for its primary and all its secondary endpoints;
  2. Aardvark Therapeutics (AARD) — on 10/08/2025 announced that it had alignment with the FDA on a protocol amendment to the company’s Phase 3 HERO trial of ARD-101 for the treatment of hyperphagia associated with PWS to change the minimum age of eligibility to participate in the trial from 13 to 10 years old;
  3. ConSynance Therapeutics, — its CSTI-500 will be initiating phase 2 development for PWS in 2025;
  4. Neuren Pharmaceuticals (OTCPK:NURPF) — its NNZ-2591 is in preclinical development;
  5. OT4B — this French company is focused on development of its oxytocin in treatment of PWS, which is in an uncertain stage of development; and
  6. Rhythm Pharmaceuticals (RYTM) — plans to initiate an exploratory Phase 2 study with higher doses of setmelanotide in treatment of PWS over a longer duration of 6 months.

With failure of Acadia's drug, Aardvark's ARD-101 is the only near term candidate likely to challenge VYKAT. Its trial (NCT06828861) is shown on clinicaltrials.gov as including a target of 90 patients with an 04/2026 study completion date. Its Open-Label Extension [OLE] Trial [NCT07197034] designed to assess medical problems of participants when taking ARD-101 in a long-term setting, has an 04/2027 study completion date.

Intellectual property protection

The excerpt below from slide 3 of Soleno's 08/2025 corporate presentation gives a positive spin on VYKAT's intellectual property protection:

When one checks this against its 10-K, its IP situation looks less secure:

Our patent portfolio consists of issued U.S. patents and pending U.S. applications. The 20-year expiration dates of our issued U.S. patents are between 2025 to 2035. We also have issued patents in ...[sundry countries around the world]. ... . The issued patents and pending patent applications include protection of compositions, methods of manufacturing, pharmaceutical formulations, and methods of treating aspects of PWS and Smith-Magenis syndrome [SMS].

Soleno's most important IP protections should be those applicable in the US. It is unhelpful to state that its US patents expire between 2025 to 2035 without more. Standing alone as I write in 10/2025, this could mean it has ten years or two months of protection.

Accordingly patent protection could be considered as a significant risk associated with investing in Soleno; that said, VYKAT does have an orphan drug designation in PWS entitling it to seven years of exclusivity.

Conclusion

Soleno has a weighty ~$3.5 billion market cap as I write on 10/10/2025. Its VYKAT XR came out of the gate strong with $32.7 million in net revenues for the second quarter after its 03/26/2025 FDA approval.

As noted above, Soleno has key supports for a powerful launch. Its liquidity is pristine; its label is supportive; it has several years ahead with no approved competition in sight. Significantly, it has stratospheric peak sales projections ranging up to Goldman Sachs' recent $3 billion in peak sales by 2035.

Soleno may become a powerhouse. The 11 Wall Street analysts covering the stock are all bullish. Their price targets range from $106 to $145, with an average of $119 for an 84% upside. The Soleno story is definitely compelling.

As I read the situation, it is now up to management to execute. The somewhat feckless remarks of CFO MacKaness quoted above give me some pause in this regard. However, the VYKAT story is so powerful that I am still rating it as a "Buy".

data.title: AI Bubble: Circular Funding And Intense Competitive Rivalry
data.description:
You have likely heard by now about the circular funding of AI capex.

  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) issuing warrants to OpenAI in exchange for OpenAI buying AMD chips
  • Nvidia (NVDA) investing $100B in OpenAI as OpenAI deploys much of the funding into buying NVDA chips
  • Oracle (ORCL) buying NVDA chips to service a big contract with OpenAI
  • NVDA potentially investing in XAI
  • XAI investing in NVDA chips

Bloomberg sums it up quite nicely into a graphic.

Basically, it is the same capital sloshing around between these companies, creating what appears to be investment of unprecedented scale.

Bears point to this circle as being reminiscent of Cisco's (CSCO) vendor financing during the internet dot-com bubble of 1999, in which Cisco would finance the dot-com companies' purchases of its equipment/services.

We all know how disastrous that wound up.

AI bulls dismiss this analogy by pointing to the vast difference between the megacaps of the AI world compared to the startups of the dot-com era. Surely these massive companies are better able to make good on their end of the deals.

I am unconvinced that companies being larger offers any sort of protection. Beyond the Cisco analogy, there are plenty of other instances in which vendor financing has not worked out well.

Plug Power (PLUG) provided a form of vendor financing to Amazon (AMZN) to sell it hydrogen.

CNBC reports on Plug's deal with Amazon:

"Amazon said Plug will supply 10,950 tons of green hydrogen per year for its transportation and building operations starting in 2025. Amazon expects Plug to provide enough green hydrogen to power 30,000 forklifts or 800 long-haul trucks. As part of the deal, Plug said it granted the e-commerce giant a warrant to buy up to 16 million shares at an exercise price of $22.9841 for the first 9 million shares. Amazon agreed to spend $2.1 billion on Plug products over the seven-year term of the contract in order for its warrants to fully vest."

If PLUG's share price had held up, the profit from these warrants would have paid for a substantial portion of AMZN's hydrogen purchase. Today, however, it seems the warrants are worth close to nothing.

This was just a drop in the bucket for AMZN, but it is just an example of how vendor financing can fail even when the recipient is extremely solvent.

Some of the problem with the AI infrastructure boom is certainly that these deals are circular, but another portion of the problem is that the buyers of the hype product cannot afford what they are buying.

It felt a lot more sustainable when it was Microsoft and Google using their massive cash flows to finance their buildout of AI infrastructure.

It feels a lot less sustainable now that the billions upon billions of capex have expanded to companies that do not have enough cash flow to pay for it or, in some cases, don't really have any cashflow.

  • Oracle has dipped into negative cashflow on its AI capex
  • OpenAI has more capex than cashflow
  • CoreWeave and XAI cannot fuel their capex with cashflow

To me this is reminiscent of the housing bubble circa 2005. Homes were being sold to people who absolutely could not afford them.

The home purchases were financed with what were called NINJA loans or (no income, no job, no assets) at very high loan-to-value.

Just as the banks were underwriting on the prospect that maybe those individuals would get a job, the vendor financing of AI infrastructure sales to these companies is predicated on the idea that they will someday become profitable.

I think that is a much more nebulous concept than it initially appears. The financial success of companies like NVDA or Constellation (CEG) is already here. These companies have grown earnings by extraordinary percentages, which I think has given a false impression that AI is already profitable.

These are labeled as AI stocks, so the market might see their profitability as AI being profitable, but they are not actually a representation of AI making money. If we examine the industry structure, I think it's clear that most of the profits are in the suppliers of AI infrastructure rather than the AI itself. Having strong suppliers such as NVDA is actually a bad thing for AI profitability.

For complex situations like this, it can be helpful to turn to classic business strategy models like Porter's 5 forces.

Each of these factors impacts the long-run margins of the industry. Let's briefly discuss their implications for the profitability of AI.

So far, AI itself has made very little money, at least compared to the amount of capital invested in it. Things like customer subscriptions to ChatGPT represent true AI revenues.

The lion's share of the money made so far is by the suppliers

  • Chips
  • Data centers
  • Electricity providers

In each category there are relatively few suppliers.

Chips are mostly NVDA and Broadcom (AVGO) with a little bit of AMD and perhaps some others on the fringe that are trying to break in.

Data centers are either provided by the major data center companies like Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty (DLR), or the hyperscalers are vertically integrating by building their own.

Electricity suppliers are increasingly sparse the more near-term you want the electricity. In a 15-year window, one could build their own plants or contract to have them built. However, in the next 5 years there will be a clear shortage of power. One can attempt to buy the tiny bit of spare capacity from the grid or use power purchase agreements (PPAs) with concierge energy providers. On the time scale AI companies are looking to power up, they are really stuck dealing with the location specific utility and companies like Constellation or Clearway Energy (CWEN), which are increasingly enjoying being able to charge remarkably high prices for power.

Getting back to Porter's 5 forces, high negotiating power of suppliers is a bad thing for industry margins.

Substitution is an unclear force at this time because it is not yet known exactly what functions the AI will perform. The capacity for substitution will likely vary by function, but I don't see it being a particularly threatening force to the industry.

New supply, however, is a major threat to AI margins. The more different platforms invest in building out their AI, the more competitive rivalry there will be in the industry. Each time there is a new announcement of a company investing 10s of billions into AI, the AI stocks seem to rise, which is baffling to me. More investment from competitors is unequivocally a bad thing for industry margins.

Finally, regarding buyer power, it is somewhat hard to read because sales of the AI itself are still in a fledgling state. Ultimately, buyer power will likely depend on how fungible the various AI functions are. If 5 different AI providers offer the same functionality, it will be quite easy for customers and potential customers to go with the lowest-price provider.

The holy grail that the AI companies seem to be seeking is AI fulfillment of a key job function that somehow cannot be replicated by peers. Perhaps there is room to do this through network externalities, software ecosystem lock-in, or other similar gating. For example, Google Sheets can do nearly everything that Excel can do, but because so many systems are integrated with Excel and so many workers were trained in Excel, it retains a strong customer base.

So, I see 3 main challenges to AI margins:

  • High supplier negotiating power.
  • High existing rivalry with many companies investing vast sums of capital to compete
  • New entrants due to ample funding and the threat of lower barriers to entry (such as what DeepSeek threatened with its low cost of training)

The pot of gold at the end of the rainbow

All of these hundreds of billions of capital investment seem to be with a goal of winning the AI arms race. It's as if there is an embedded assumption that being the first to have a "complete" AI buildout will somehow own the world.

I don't doubt that there is some value here. I just think that both the magnitude and distribution of AI revenues remain highly uncertain. There are clear competitive threats in the industry vertical, which imply margins will be moderate at best.

It feels like a bubble to me. Extraordinarily high multiples for businesses with unclear margins, unclear revenues, and a high level of competition are not sustainable.

Perhaps the most damning aspect to me is the extreme capital intensity with no clear timeline on when these massive investments are going to generate a return.

When might the bubble pop?

In the early excitement phases of a craze, there is plenty of capital wanting to dive in. As time goes on, the major players have to stretch a bit more to find that capital. That, in my opinion, is what we are seeing with the circular financing (or vendor financing).

Looking at history, when investment into a craze is fueled by high-risk or circular sources, it tends to correspond to the end of the cycle.

  • Plug Power vendor financing was the tail end of the hydrogen hype
  • NINJA loans signaled the end of the housing bubble
  • Cisco vendor financing peaked the internet bubble.

Implications for investment

The true AI companies will have to find a revenue stream that justifies the massive capex.

The AI suppliers may experience declining revenues as the current volume of capex is not long-term sustainable.

The stock market may find it increasingly hard to justify nosebleed multiples. Many trillions of dollars will have to deflate from market caps.

Will it cause collateral damage to uninvolved stocks, or will capital finally flow to the companies that are currently being ignored?

data.title: How To Build A $500,000 Low-Stress Retirement Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction:

Markets are making fresh all-time highs with almost regular frequency. It seems that nothing can stop the market, at least until it crosses the psychological SPX7000 level. Most of the advance after the tariff tantrums (back in April) has been fueled by the AI boom. However, more recently, the sentiment has been up due to the start of interest rate cuts by the Fed and expectations of at least two more cuts this year and some more next year. The AI-fueled boom has disproportionately benefited a select group of tech companies. The so-called Magnificent Seven have dominated the gains of the S&P 500 in 2024/2025, but a new set of companies has emerged to lead the gains as well, such as Broadcom (AVGO), Oracle (ORCL), and Palantir (PLTR).

So, the big question is: will this rally continue to build, or is it a bubble about to burst? No one can predict the future accurately, so we won't even try. But fortunately, retirees and near-term retirees do not need to answer that question to be successful.

Sure, they face the problem of risk from "sequence of returns". The sequence of returns is the risk of the market entering into a deep correction as you enter retirement. This means you run the risk of having large negative returns in the first couple of years of retirement. Along with withdrawals, this can deplete your retirement nest egg significantly and thereby impact long-term retirement security. But there are ways to deal with and protect yourself from the risk of sequence, which we will discuss towards the end.

We believe it is never a good idea to keep a lot of cash or stay uninvested. The simple reason is that cash earns little, and whatever it earns rarely exceeds the rate of inflation. So, we always like to stay invested.

How to Retire on $500,000?

Let's say you have saved a sum of $500,000 for your retirement portfolio, or you are in the process of saving that tidy sum and are a few years away from that goal. $500,000 is not a magic number; you could and should be targeting more like a million dollars (or more). These are personal numbers, and one size does not fit all. The retirement capital should be determined based on your income during the last five years prior to retirement. Usually, 8-12 times your annual income (just prior to retirement) may be appropriate. So, whether you are already retired or planning and investing for a retirement income, this article is for you.

We will attempt to provide some fresh ideas and strategic planning that you can use to build your own personalized investment or retirement portfolio. Our aim is to formulate a balanced, somewhat risk-averse, income-producing, and growth-oriented portfolio with just a few funds. If you are interested in going over how you can budget based on a $500,000 portfolio, we recommend reading one of our recent articles here. In this article, we will not go over those details; instead, we will focus on some strategies that you could deploy to invest this amount and generate sufficient income and long-term growth. In addition, we will provide a strategy to deal with the risk of "sequence of returns", as that is a valid concern for most folks about to retire.

What is a SWAN portfolio, and how can you design your own SWAN portfolio?

In the investment world, a SAWN portfolio stands for a "Sleep Well At Night" type of portfolio, which provides a hassle-free and relatively stress-free investment exposure. The stock market is inherently volatile. It goes through frequent cycles of boom and bust. For most ordinary investors, it is almost impossible to know when a cycle changes, and they are often caught in these stressful situations. Even when the market is doing well, the ride is usually very bumpy.

Further, a truly 'simplified' SWAN portfolio should have very few investments without compromising on diversification. One can obviously form a SWAN portfolio with a collection of individual stocks, but it will lose simplicity. So, to keep it simple, we would invest in a set of diversified funds. After all, it is easier to monitor five or six funds compared to 30 individual stocks. The advantage with funds is that you can get instant diversification, as they normally hold tens (if not hundreds) of individual stocks and securities. f

We will describe one such portfolio in the following sections.

How to Build a Retirement Portfolio of $500,000

Investment Portfolio of 6 Funds:

The portfolio described below can be used in retirement as well as for reaching your retirement goals.

Investment Goals and Objectives:

  • High Income: The portfolio should generate at least 5% income.
  • Decent Growth: It should not be all about income. The portfolio should also provide at least 3-4% growth over and above the targeted 5% income. This should translate into 9% to 10% overall growth (if all income were reinvested). That is also the historical average annualized return of the stock market over a very long term.
  • Lower Volatility and Drawdowns: The portfolio should provide at least 20% to 30% less drawdowns during times of corrections and panic.
  • Keep it simple: Invest in no more than 5 to 7 funds (max 10). This recommendation is for passive investors. Sure, active investors could add some individual stocks as well. This article is focused on passive investors.

We will form our portfolio based on the following SIX funds as per the allocations specified:

  • Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) - 20%
  • Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) - 20%
  • JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) – 25%
  • JPMorgan Hedged Equity Laddered Overlay ETF (HELO) – 12.5%
  • Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) OR PGIM AAA CLO ETF (PAAA) - 15%
  • iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF (SHY) - 7.5%.

Table-1: Portfolio of 6 funds

Backtesting Results from Jan. 2014 until Sep. 2025:

Some of the portfolio's components (JEPQ and JAAA/PAAA) have a relatively short history. So, to backtest with a significant time period, we will need to replace them with some similar funds that have longer histories. We replace PAAA/JAAA with Cash (even though it is a poor substitute, and we feel PAAA or JAAA would have yielded better results). We also replaced JEPQ with Columbia Seligman Prem Technology Growth Fund (STK) for backtesting purposes. They are different but still follow the same basic approach, with each having more than 50% allocation to technology.

A third component, HELO, has a short history, too, but it is easy to replace with its sister fund, JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund (JHQAX), which is very similar (with some subtle differences) and has been in existence since 2013. JHQAX and HELO share the same fund managers and follow a similar strategy. Please note that JHQAX is a mutual fund, while HELO is an ETF.

We also include in backtesting a hypothetical Stocks/Bonds/Cash portfolio with 60% stocks, 30% bonds, and 10% cash allocation.

Table-1A: Performance comparison from Jan. 2014 until Sep. 30, 2025

Details about the selected Funds:

(SCHD) Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF and (ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund:

Together, these two funds will take a major share of 40% of the portfolio, and will serve as the foundation of our portfolio. SCHD is an ETF, while ADX is a non-leveraged CEF (closed-end fund). SCHD provides income based on what it earns, while ADX (being a CEF) provides 'managed' income, as it is committed to providing roughly 8% of its average NAV on a yearly basis. Together, they will provide a roughly 6% yield. Please note that both funds use large-cap stocks from the S&P 500 and may have overlap in holdings.

SCHD is one of the most popular dividend ETFs. It is generally invested in large blue-chip dividend-paying stocks from the S&P 500 index. The fund offers wide diversification, exposure to large blue-chip dividend stocks, moderately high-income yield, very low fees, and an impressive record of dividend growth. SCHD currently pays a 3.77% yield (TTM yield of 3.67%, 30-day SEC yield being 3.83%). SCHD's dividend payout can vary every quarter based on its underlying holdings. The fund's fee structure is very low at 0.06%, which means only $6 is charged (annually) on a capital base of $10,000. The fund's official benchmark is the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index. Also, SCHD has a solid record of growing its dividends, and the dividend growth has been 10.4% during the last 5 years.

The ADX fund also invests in large-cap blue-chip stocks from the S&P 500, both dividend-paying and non-dividend-paying. Distributions are paid mostly from investment income and realized capital gains on a 'managed' basis. The fund has a very long and successful history of paying consistent distributions. The fee structure is reasonable at 0.64% (in comparison to other CEFs of a similar nature).

The Top 10 holdings of SCHD are AbbVie (ABBV), Merck (MRK), Amgen (AMGN), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Cisco (CSCO), Verizon (VZ), Altria (MO), ConocoPhillips (COP), Chevron (CVX), and Home Depot (HD).

The Top 10 holdings of ADX are: Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta (META), Broadcom (AVGO), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Adams Natural Resources (PEO), and Netflix (NFLX).

(JEPQ) JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF:

While our first two funds covered the stocks from the S&P 500 and focused on providing income, our next pick is aimed at both income and higher growth. Retirees need growth, as their portfolios need to last 30 years or more. Inflation is a constant factor that is chipping away at your spending power. Without growth, most portfolios will not last 30 years.

JEPQ is offered by the JPMorgan group and invests primarily in Nasdaq-100 stocks. So, in that sense, it is concentrated in technology and high-growth stocks. Since, for usual blue-chip dividend-paying stocks, we already have SCHD and ADX, JEPQ will provide the exposure to high-growth stocks. At the same time, it provides a high level of income on a monthly basis that not only provides flexibility to withdraw for retirees' expenses, but part of it can be reinvested.

The fund writes covered calls on part of its underlying portfolio to generate high income while also providing some limited downside protection. However, nothing comes free; this also means that the upside of its underlying holdings will be somewhat limited, and growth performance will be subpar compared to the Nasdaq-100 index, especially in a bull market.

The fund uses a rather complex and unique approach of using ELNs (Equity-Linked-Notes) instead of writing actual calls. As per the fund, the ELNs that the fund invests in are derivative instruments that are designed to offer the economic benefit/loss of the covered calls written on the Nasdaq index. It writes monthly covered calls on the Nasdaq Index using weekly tranches with OTM (out-of-money) strikes, which allows it to capture a reasonable (but not all) of the upside while earning decent premiums to generate income. The 30-day SEC yield is 9.45%, while the 12-month rolling yield is 11.03%.

The top holdings in the ETF are large technology stocks, for example, NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOGL), Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), Netflix (NFLX), and JPMorgan US Govt MMkt (MGMXX). The top 10 holdings represent roughly 47% of the assets.

(HELO) JPMorgan Hedged Equity Laddered Overlay ETF

Or (JHQAX) JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund:

This fund is offered by the JPMorgan group and not only provides exposure to U.S. large-cap stocks but also provides a hedging mechanism. Investors could either use the JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund (JHQAX) or its similar ETF version, JPMorgan Hedged Equity Laddered Overlay ETF (HELO). JHQAX has nearly 12 years of history, while HELO is a pretty new ETF and has less than two years of history. Please note that there are some subtle differences between JHQAX and HELO, and their performance may not match exactly. These hedged equity funds aim to provide decent growth and protection against large market downturns. For example, in the year 2022, JHQAX lost only about 8% while S&P500 lost roughly 16%. But it has also underperformed the SP500 over the years, but nearly matches the performance of a 60:40 stock/Bond portfolio or any balanced index fund. This kind of fund helps investors to participate in equity market gains while mitigating risk in a declining market.

(PAAA) PGIM AAA CLO ETF

Or (JAAA) Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF:

We are allocating 15% of the portfolio to this asset class, and the main purpose is to provide risk-adjusted high yield (higher than cash). We provide two choices above, with PAAA having slightly higher quality underlying assets than JAAA but with a lower yield. These funds primarily invest in high-quality AAA-rated CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations). These CLOs are senior corporate loans (floating-rate loans), but being AAA-rated, they are considered highly safe and have very low credit and default risk. In fact, there have been zero defaults (from these assets) during the last 30 years. Due to these reasons, the volatility is generally very low. These funds generally provide low correlation to traditional fixed income asset classes while providing lower volatility and downgrade risk.

Both PAAA and JAAA's portfolios are nearly 100% composed of high-quality AAA-rated CLO tranches (with PAAA having a slight edge). In other words, their portfolios hold the highest quality tranches of CLOs and are designed to deliver stable income to their investors.

We are allocating 15% of the portfolio to this fund, and the main purpose is to provide diversification to an alternative and high-quality asset with predictable income. Please note that the income is likely to fall as the Fed cuts interest rates. In addition, since this is a cash-like (not exactly though) investment, overall returns are likely to be much lower than stocks.

(SHY) iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF

Or (SGOV) iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF:

We are allocating a small portion of the portfolio to short-term Treasury funds. This will help in keeping the overall portfolio conservative and having lower volatility. This will also provide a retiree with flexibility in the form of emergency cash. Both of these ETFs are short-term Treasury bonds, while SGOV is very short-term (duration 1-3 months). As an alternative, one could also use laddered bank CDs with similar yields. We can use either fund, but a mix of both will be preferable. Currently, the yields for SGOV and SHY are reasonably attractive at 4.28% and 3.88%, respectively. However, yields are expected to fall gradually as the Fed will likely continue reducing the interest rates. The fund SGOV is almost as good as cash and will protect the overall portfolio from stock market drawdowns.

How to Deal with "Risk of Sequence of Returns"?

This is relevant for folks who may be retiring now or within the next few months to a year and plan to withdraw income to meet expenses.

To avoid the risk of sequence of returns (defined earlier), we recommend using a "rotational" strategy on at least on part of your portfolio. We will suggest one such strategy that not only produces income but is also likely to provide market-matching total returns. While it does that, it will provide the necessary protection from any deep corrections in the stock market. Here is the strategy:

We will invest in four primary securities plus one security as the hedging security. We will invest in the four primary securities on a continuous basis (each with 25% allocation), but would replace a security with the hedging security whenever it has performed worse (over the past few months) than the risk-free return provided by Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury). We check this on a monthly basis. This is explained in more detail a bit later.

This strategy is not a buy-and-hold type. Instead, it requires monthly trading, but mostly one or two trades a month. For this reason, it will be much more preferable to have this bucket inside a tax-deferred type of account.

The following are the four securities for regular use, plus one security as the hedging security.

Table-3: List of 5 securities:

Ticker

Name

Purpose

(ADX)

Adams Diversified Equity Fund

Note: QLD is an ultra QQQ (Nasdaq-100), and it aims to provide 2x returns of QQQ after fees. We do not recommend QLD as a buy-and-hold investment, but this is inside a rotation portfolio, which means it will be held only when it is performing well. More conservative investors could replace it with QQQ.

The detailed steps to determine the monthly rotation:

Step-1: At the end of the month, calculate the average performance based on 3-month and 7-month periods for all four regular securities. The performance would mean total returns, including any dividends/distributions. Also, calculate the returns provided by risk-free Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury) over the same period.

Step-2: Compare the performance of each of the regular four securities (ADX, RQI, KYN, QLD) with the risk-free return provided by Cash. If it is worse, then replace that security with IEF (hedging security) for the next month.

Step-3: We will keep the investment from Step-2 for the entire next month.

Step-4: At the end of the following month, we will repeat the process (from Step-1 through 3).

Backtested Performance – Hedging/Rotational Bucket:

In addition to providing hedging and market-beating growth, this rotation strategy can also provide a significant amount of monthly income (our rough estimate at 6%). Three of the four securities selected provide high income (close to 8%), while the fourth one, QLD, provides almost no income. Also, due to rotation, at times, the hedging security with a lower yield may replace the regular security with a higher yield. So, on average, we expect the portfolio income to be roughly 6%.

Risks:

All investments carry some level of risk. Please consider the likely risks and your own risk tolerance before making any investments.

The portfolio mentioned in this article has roughly 7.5% cash equivalents and another 15% allocation to an 'AAA' rated CLO fund that provides higher income than cash but adds some risk. The rest of the roughly 75% portfolio is mostly equity but highly diversified. Some portion is hedged as well, but the majority is not. So, 75% of the portfolio will have similar or slightly less volatility than the broader market. However, this portfolio provides 6% plus income that can smooth the ride during market corrections.

Concluding Thoughts:

This article is not about whether $500,000 is enough to retire on. You could read our previous article on that topic (link provided earlier). This article discusses how to successfully invest $500,000 for retirement income and growth to last over 30 years or more of retirement. It assumes that no more than 5% to 6% of income is withdrawn annually.

To provide simplicity for passive investors, we have recommended a fund-based portfolio with only six funds. Out of these six funds, one is a cash-like security, while a second one provides higher income than cash but some added risk, and is still relatively safe. We have also included a fund that provides hedging, resulting in significantly reduced volatility and drawdowns, but also lower growth than the S&P 500. Overall, 65% of the portfolio is directed towards income and growth from equities.

As a bonus, for those who may be interested, we provide a rotation-based strategy to specifically deal with the risk of the sequence of returns in the initial years of retirement.

data.title: First Majestic Silver: Soaring As Silver Breaches All-Time Highs
data.description:
First Majestic Silver (NYSE:AG)(TSX:AG:CA) is a Canadian-based miner with four operating mines in Mexico.

Gold recently smashed through the $4,000 barrier, and silver is in hot pursuit, having just breached its own all-time high - which it had reached on the 18th of January 1980, when the Hunt brothers famously cornered the silver market.

It would take a brave creature to bet against a silver miner in this environment - and this Acorn Accumulator is not willing to take that side of the wager.

Interestingly, over the past 15 years, First Majestic Silver has been predictably cyclical and, despite occasional outperformance, has overall lagged the silver price.

First Majestic Silver has let investors down at times, and the ongoing tax dispute with Mexican authorities - which I'll touch on later in this article - has kept some investors on the sidelines compared with other silver miners. But honestly, there is still money to be made here, and the current environment feels right for a comeback. With shares already up more than 120% year-to-date, I think AG still has more room to run up even further.

Assets And Production Trends

Silver Production has been on a three-year downward trend, with gold output declining for two consecutive years. However, the acquisition of a 70% stake in the Los Gatos mine at the start of 2025 is set to significantly boost silver production this year, even as gold output is expected to edge lower still.

Los Gatos

Los Gatos is a modern underground silver mine that has been in production since 2019. Additionally, it has great exploration potential as it sits on an underexplored and highly prospective 103,000 hectares land package. Los Gatos is expected to produce between 8.0 and 8.6 million silver ounces in 2025.

Santa Elena

2025 silver production is expected to me marginally higher compared 2024.

San Dimas

2025 silver production is expected to be better than 2024, but still lower than the period between 2019 and 2023.

La Encantada

2025 silver production is expected to arrest four consecutive years of decline.

Q2 Earnings

First Majestic Silver just posted the kind of quarter long-time shareholders have been waiting for, producing 7.9 million silver equivalent ounces following the Los Gatos acquisition - which was a 48% increase over Q2'2024. The realized silver price averaged $34.62 per ounce (up 24% over the prior comparative period in Q2'2024), and that combination of higher prices and volumes pushed revenue to a record $264.2 million, which is nearly double last year's Q2 level. Net income also swung from a $48 million loss in Q2 last year to a $56.6 million profit in Q2 this year.

First Majestic Silver's balance sheet now looks bulletproof for a mid-tier producer. AG ended Q2'25 with $510 million in cash, its strongest financial position to date - and also has an undrawn $100 million credit facility, giving it significant liquidity headroom. AG has indicated that they aim to become the world's largest primary silver producer, which tells me that investors should expect further acquisitions to come. The three consecutive years of declining silver output between 2022 and 2024 underscored the need to supplement production, either through new asset acquisition or by advancing exploration and development across existing mines. I think this strong liquidity position gives First Majestic genuine flexibility to act quickly when acquisition or expansion opportunities arise. Furthermore, First Mint, the company's retail bullion arm, continues to gain traction too, with sales more than doubling to $7.8 million.

I think investors would agree that for years, First Majestic Silver has been a story that rarely lived up to its own hype. However, with record cash, rising output, and some operational momentum finally - and with the silver price having just breached its prior all-time high - the narrative around First Majestic Silver is starting to look a lot more interesting.

Future Catalysts

First Majestic Silver has been leaning hard into exploration this year, launching one of its biggest drill programs yet - with around 255k meters planned and 28 rigs currently active across its mines. I think it is encouraging that they are putting this much focus into growing production organically - as there is a real risk of overpaying for new assets in this environment of record-high silver prices.

The main excitement is at Santa Elena in Mexico. Drilling there has uncovered three promising new zones - Navidad, Santo Niño, and Winter - that could end up being as large, or even larger, than Hermitano, the deposit that has supplied much of Santa Elena's ore in recent years.

In Nevada, First Majestic has stepped up work at Jerritt Canyon, adding another drill rig to map out new gold-bearing structures. This mine has struggled in the past, but AG is hoping that fresh exploration data will help build a clearer case for a restart down the line.

Elsewhere in AG's portfolio, steady drilling continues at Cerro Los Gatos and San Dimas - aimed at simply replacing what has been mined and extending overall mine life.

Tax Dispute

First Majestic Silver has been engaged in a protracted tax dispute with Mexico's tax authority. The issue stems from a previous agreement on how the company's silver sales were taxed, which Mexican authorities later challenged. This led to large tax reassessments and the withholding of VAT refunds that First Majestic was expecting to receive. First Majestic argues that the actions of the Mexican tax authority violate international trade and investment protections, and it has taken the matter to an international arbitration tribunal. At one stage, the Mexican authorities were seeking $534 million in back taxes from First Majestic. And now First Majestic is also pursuing a claim for damages of $500 million, while simultaneously disputing the taxes and the blocked VAT refund of $80 million.

Valuation

I have chosen Pan American Silver, Endeavour Silver Corp., Hecla Mining, Silvercorp Metals, Coeur Mining, and Fresnillo as peers for First Majestic Silver because they all focus heavily on silver production and operate in similar regions, mostly in the Americas. I have deliberately included a mix of mid-tier and larger producers to give a balanced view - some peers reflect First Majestic's current scale, while the bigger ones provide a benchmark for what the company could aspire to as it grows.

I like using FWD EV/EBITDA ratios when valuing miners, as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the earnings that they are forecast to generate. Over the past five years, First Majestic Silver traded at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 25% above peers, on average. Currently, that ratio sits just 3% above peers - suggesting the AG is undervalued on a comparative basis - and which equates to a price target of $16.87 (21% above current prices).

I think the current valuation reflects recent shareholder disappointment from a longer-term trend of declining production and a lingering tax overhang. I must admit that it is tough arguing this point with a straight face... considering that shares are up nearly 120% year-to-date. However, shares of AG have not repriced as strongly as peers, suggesting investors have been somewhat cautious - but with production at an inflection point and a strong balance sheet, I think AG's prospects are looking up.

AG currently trades nearly one standard deviation below its own five-year average FWD EV/EBITDA premium relative to peers - suggesting an attractive entry point for investors with high conviction in silver.

Risks

I think there are still risks that investors should keep in mind. The ongoing tax dispute with Mexican authorities remains unresolved and could materially impact cash flow if the outcome is unfavourable. Operationally, while Los Gatos provides a meaningful boost, production across other mines remains somewhat uneven, and exploration success is never guaranteed. Although I see it as an encouraging signal that silver prices closed this week above $50, with implied 1-week volatility at 27 versus the 6-month average of 22 - a big retracement lower in silver prices could cause investors in AG to take a big knock if they are not able to stomach that volatility.

The Technical Take

First Majestic Silver shares are exhibiting really strong momentum - and shares are trading well above the 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving average. The RSI indicator is also in bullish territory, albeit nearing an oversold level.

Conclusion

Yes, the Mexican tax dispute is still a cloud, but the setup looks too good to ignore. Silver just breached its prior all-time highs, Los Gatos just turbocharged output, and exploration rigs are buzzing across Mexico and Nevada. Cash is stacking up, the balance sheet is solid, and operations are finally firing on all cylinders. AG's relative valuation vs peers also points to this being a decent entry point. I have a Buy rating with a price target of $16.87 (21% above current prices, which agrees with the price target implied by the historic average FWD EV/EBITDA multiple vs peers - as detailed in the valuation segment).

data.title: 5 Best REITs To Buy In October 2025
data.description:
The big news of this past month was that the Fed cut interest rates for the first time since December 2024.

Not only that, but they strongly implied in their commentary that more rate cuts would likely be needed in the near term. Here is what the Fed's Governor, Michelle Bowman, said:

"It's a lot easier to support the labor market by lowering the federal funds rate than it is to fix it after it's broken. It is time for the Committee to act decisively and proactively to address decreasing labor market dynamism and emerging signs of fragility." (emphasis added)

And they are right to be nervous about the job market. In September, private payrolls were expected to rise by 50,000, but they dropped by 32,000 instead. A huge miss.

As a result, the debt market is now predicting 5-6 additional rate cuts by the end of next year, bringing interest rates to the high 2s or low 3s:

This would be very good news for REITs (VNQ), particularly those that are heavily leveraged.

As a reminder, REITs have been in a bear market for 3+ years, causing their valuations to drop to historic lows, and this was almost entirely driven by the surge in interest rates of 2022 and 2023.

But if REITs crashed due to the rate hikes, this also means that they could now strongly recover as rates are cut, and those presenting the most upside potential are typically small-cap REITs with higher leverage and deeply discounted valuations.

Here are 5 such REITs to consider in October as we enter another cycle of monetary easing:

Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH)

Armada is a small-cap that focuses primarily on mixed-use properties, often combining retail, residential, and office under one roof. These are true class A trophy assets, typically located in walkable, high-barrier-to-entry locations of growing Sunbelt markets:

Its properties are performing well, enjoying high occupancy rates and steady rent growth. But what has heavily weighted down the company's valuation is its balance sheet, which is more leveraged than most other REITs, with a 7.4x Debt-to-EBITDA. This clearly concerns the market, and it explains the crash in the company's share price:

As a result, it now trades at just 6.5x FFO, an estimated 50% discount to its net asset value, and offers an 8% dividend yield that's well covered.

Such low valuations would only make sense if we remained in a higher interest rate environment and Armada turned into a value trap as it faced steadily rising interest expense.

But as interest rates are now cut down to materially lower levels, even as Armada also further deleverages its balance sheet, we expect a strong recovery over the coming years.

As it finishes its development projects, its debt-to-EBITDA is expected to drop to the mid-5s, which is far more reasonable, and this, coupled with 5-6 additional rate cuts, could easily push Armada back to 10-12x FFO.

This would result in 50-100% upside potential from here, and even at that level, it would still trade at a steep discount to most REITs. While you wait, you earn an 8% dividend yield.

Insiders clearly believe that the stock is undervalued, as they bought over $4 million worth of shares over the past year alone.

Whitestone REIT (WSR)

Whitestone owns mostly service-oriented neighborhood retail centers in rapidly growing Sunbelt markets like Phoenix, Austin, Dallas, and Houston:

Its properties enjoy sector-leading growth prospects, and the management has guided for steady 3-5% same-property NOI growth going forward.

This strong organic growth, coupled with some new accretive acquisitions, has the potential to result in 5-7% annual FFO per share growth going forward.

Even then, the market has heavily discounted Whitestone, primarily due to its balance sheet, which is still reasonable but more leveraged than most of its peers.

Its Debt-to-EBITDA is 7.1x, and this has concerned the market in recent years. The good news is that the company has made significant progress in deleveraging, with its Debt-to-EBITDA already dropping 3 turns, and they expect to reach 6.5x by the end of next year, which, coupled with significant rate cuts, should help their market sentiment.

We estimate their NAV at about $18 per share, and yet, you get to buy it today at just around $12, representing a 30% discount on desirable assets that are undersupplied and enjoying steady growth.

While you wait for the upside, you earn a 4.4% dividend yield, which, coupled with 5-7% FFO per share growth, has the potential to earn you double-digit total returns.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / OTCPK:BSRTF)

BSR REIT is a very similar story. It is also a small-cap, primarily focused on Dallas, Houston, and Austin, but instead of buying neighborhood centers, it owns apartment communities:

Just like Whitestone, BSR has enjoyed sector-leading growth, and yet, it trades at a lower valuation than most of its peers, largely because of its more heavily leveraged balance sheet.

Its near 50% loan-to-value is still reasonable for such defensive assets, but it is higher than its peers.

However, the REIT is very prudent in that it retains a significant portion of its cash flow and has been selling assets, using some of those proceeds to deleverage and buy back shares to create value for shareholders.

Today, it is priced at an estimated 25% discount based on today's net asset value. But as interest rates are cut down further, even as its rent growth accelerates in 2026/2027, we expect its NAV to surge.

Based on its forward normalized 2026/2027 NAV, we think that the current discount is closer to 30-40%, and as conditions improve, we expect that gap to close, which could lead to up to 50% upside.

While you wait, you earn a 4.4% dividend yield, and the REIT keeps growing steadily.

Clipper Realty (CLPR)

Clipper has some similarities with BSR in that it is also a small REIT that focuses on affordable apartment communities that are performing well, but it is heavily discounted due to its balance sheet.

The difference is that Clipper focuses on NYC, and its balance sheet is even more heavily leveraged than that of BSR. On the flip side, it has enjoyed stronger rent growth in recent years due to the limited new supply in its markets, and its valuation is even cheaper than that of BSR.

Its Debt-to-EBITDA is sky-high at well over 10x, and its valuation is very low, trading at just 9x FFO and an estimated 50% discount to NAV.

As such, it is a highly leveraged play on interest rates at this point.

If rates are cut significantly over the next year, as the debt market is predicting, Clipper could see its share price surge 50-100% from here. But if rates remain higher for longer, Clipper could turn into a value trap and be forced to cut its dividend, currently yielding 10%.

It is a riskier play for investors with a higher risk tolerance, but we like the risk-to-reward, especially potentially going into a new easing cycle.

Macerich (MAC)

Macerich owns the highest quality malls in the US, even better than those of Simon Property Group (SPG) or Tanger (SKT).

They are located in more urban locations with higher barriers to entry and richer demographics. They also generate much higher sales per square foot:

Even then, it trades at a steep discount relative to its peers, and it is almost entirely due to its weaker balance sheet.

But the REIT is rapidly deleveraging its balance sheet by selling assets, handing back keys to lenders in select cases, retaining most of its cash flow, and growing its rental income.

By the end of 2028, they expect their Debt-to-EBITDA to drop to just 6.5x, which, coupled with significant rate cuts, could push the REIT to much higher levels.

We expect 2028 FFO per share to come in at right around $2 per share, which at a 15x multiple (in line with Tanger's current multiple) would push the share price to $30. That's nearly double today's price.

Closing Note

The Fed is back at cutting interest rates, and the cheapest REITs with the most leverage often offer the most upside potential.

They are set to win the most as interest rates are cut further, and we are positioning our portfolio accordingly at High Yield Landlord.
