data.title: Mosaic's Pullback On Phosphate Production Struggles Provides An Appealing Entry Point
data.description:
We last looked at Mosaic (NYSE:MOS) in June, and I downgraded them from strong buy to buy on the basis of the stock having surged to near its 52-week high. In general though, I’ve been bullish about Mosaic’s fertilizer business, with expectations that between the expected recovery of Brazil’s economy and the generally high commodity prices, they’re well positioned to make good money, even if tariffs are a complicating factor.

Friday, we saw Mosaic drop a substantial amount on the release of preliminary Q3 volumes, which while an increase quarter over quarter were somewhat below guidance because of a mechanical issue at their Riverview plant in Florida. That’s going to be the subject of today’s analysis, and whether this reflects a substantial change in the investment thesis going forward or is simply a one-off problem that may provide a cheaper entry position than we’ve had in months.

Before we get into what we know about the Q3 production, though, I think it’s important to take a look at the earnings for the first two quarters of the year, because they’re better than you’d think.

Margins Improve on High Price

Mosaic was meant to scale up volume in the second half of the year, but earnings were already rather strong as a result of high commodity prices. We can see where the margins benefited substantially already in the first half.

1st Half 2024

1st Half 2025

Net Sales

$5.5 billion

$5.6 billion

Gross Profit

$793 million

$1.0 billion

Operating Income

$406 million

$583 million

Net Income

($116 million)

$649 million

GAAP EPS

(36¢)

$2.05

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

Though revenue wasn’t up much yet, we saw the margins benefit from higher prices in both potash and phosphates, with gross margins up from 14.4% to 17.9% and operating margins up from 7.4% to 10.4%.

On top of that Mosaic went from a $368 million charge for foreign currency loss in 2024 to a $302.5 million gain in 2025, which shifted them from treading water to raking in an enviable amount of income in the first half of the year.

The potash prices have remained strong through this period, and that’s a big part of the story of the first half of 2025. The second half is meant to be about Mosaic growing their phosphate production, and those prices remain strong as well, so the hope was for earnings to get even better.

Production Growth, But Not as Much as Hoped

It’s at this point we have to look at the guidance for phosphate production/sales in Q3. During the conference call for Q2 earnings, we were told to expect sales between 1.8 million and 2.0 million tonnes for the third quarter. What we got was somewhat less, though.

Citing the mechanical issues at Riverview and the utility outages at Bartow, Mosaic was only able to come in with production of around 1.7 million tonnes. On top of that, the amount shipped was only around 1.6 million tonnes, because of the lag between production and shipment at the end of the quarter.

On the one hand, this is an increase over the production we saw in Q2, and with phosphate prices roughly where they were last quarter. Given this, there is reason to expect that the earnings aren’t going to be calamitously bad. The problem is only that the guidance was higher still, and we didn’t reach it.

This suggests Mosaic is likely to come in a bit below estimates for Q3, at least on the phosphate side of things. We do see the company remaining strong on other fronts though, with potash coming up in-line with guidance at 2.3 million tonnes, and Mosaic Fertilizantes, the Brazil business, up 25% quarter-over-quarter to 2.8 million tonnes. With margins likely to remain on the strong side because prices remain high, I’m not overly pessimistic about Q3 estimates, even if we might come in marginally below the EPS estimate of $1.06 (non-GAAP). Friday’s sell-off seems like it more than prices in that eventuality.

The market reacted quickly, and RBC Capital Markets was quick to downgrade them on the news, lowering their price target to $30.00. They expressed concern that the operational issues may persist, though Mosaic in their own statement insisted that the problems were addressed and that they expect higher production rates going forward.

That is where known fact and worst-case scenario diverge. Though the mechanical problems are surely unwelcome, Mosaic is a huge producer with a long track record, and if they say the matter has been resolved, I think that’s something to consider more than just idle gossip.

RBC sees Mosaic as needing several strong quarters of production to win back investor confidence, but with the prices that we’re seeing on the phosphate and potash market, Mosaic has an opportunity to continue winning on the earnings side of things, and that, for me, covers a multitude of sins.

Given the shipped levels on Mosaic’s other products, and the report that the mechanical problems were resolved, I think the $30.00 price target is far too low, given the earnings Mosaic has enjoyed and should continue to enjoy, mechanical problems in Q3 notwithstanding.

What to Expect in Q3 Earnings

The next possible catalyst is the Q3 earnings, which are scheduled for November 4. Friday’s volumes portend a miss, though how substantial of one is the real question here. A roughly 10% sell-off on the news suggests they’re expecting a pretty big miss here, despite the potash and the Brazil numbers both coming in on the strong side.

If the earnings are more modest, and importantly if the next conference call reiterates that the production problems are behind them, that 10% may seem like too much, and Mosaic’s anticipated earnings could justify a recovery.

While investors may have hoped that Mosaic’s production would surge up sooner, rather than later, to take advantage of the high fertilizer prices, those prices have seemed resilient, and that means that even if we don’t see the full rate of production realized until Q4, the buyers are still there to pay Mosaic better-than-normal rates.

Bullish Case for Mosaic Intact, Just With a Lower Entry Price

Missing Q3 guidance might, in the end, be exactly the sort of entry level people who are bullish about Mosaic were looking for. The company has suggested this mechanical problem is behind them, and we have a chance to get back into the stock 10% cheaper than we did the day before.

All Mosaic has really lost, after all, is 200,000 to 300,000 tonnes of phosphate in Q3. If they’re correct that the production is back where it is meant to be, the earnings and, by extension, the share price should follow. I'm upgrading this back to a strong buy because, in my view, it's quite cheap at these levels.

Investors should keep a close eye on the margins in Q3, because those will tell the tale of how bad this production problem was. If they remain resilient, as they were in the first two quarters, it should be easier still to look beyond Q3 to their higher production levels in Q4 and beyond.

Far from a crisis, the Q3 volume miss might be the opportunity for a cheaper price that the longs were waiting for. Mosaic is trading well below their book value at this point, and their GAAP earnings should remain strong irrespective of the near-term weakness. For those willing to wait for a turnaround, this news and sell-off may look like a blip, and one not to be missed.

data.title: Ferrari: Capital Markets Day Was A Huge Disappointment (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
More than two hours into Ferrari's (NYSE:RACE) highly anticipated Capital Markets Day, a room full of analysts and a webcast with thousands of investors began to feel a little weird.

They've been hearing about Ferrari's uniqueness, history, heritage, ESG plans, and previous achievements all morning, but future financial targets have yet to be disclosed.

Perhaps it was a sign of what was about to come, as the targets that were eventually disclosed were a major letdown, sending shares into their worst selloff since 2016.

I argue investors should take a wait-and-see approach before jumping to capitalize on the selloff and downgrade the stock to 'Hold', as the Capital Markets Day reveals more structural issues than I previously thought.

Ferrari's Capital Markets Day Targets Shock Investors

Let's get right to it. From 2016 to 2024, Ferrari's revenues grew at a 10% CAGR, and from 2021 to 2024, the number is 16%. Meanwhile, net income grew at 18% and 22% CAGRs, respectively.

In its 2030 targets, Ferrari is telling investors that those days are over. For 2030, they are guiding revenues at €9 billion, EBITDA at €3.6 billion, EBIT at €2.75 billion, and EPS at €11.5. These targets reflect a very disappointing 5% CAGR for revenue through 2030, and a 5.2% CAGR for EPS.

In addition, the next big thing for Ferrari was supposed to be the fully electric Elettrica. In the 2022 Capital Markets Day, they said it is expected to be 40% of deliveries by 2030, a target that they've now cut in half.

Investors were clearly caught off guard, and shares took a 16% plunge yesterday, the worst selloff since Ferrari's IPO.

Ferrari Takes Conservatism To The Extreme, But Confidence Is Low After Q2

Long-time Ferrari investors should know that the company tends to be ridiculously conservative with its guidance. Look no further than their previous Capital Markets Day in 2022.

In that one, Ferrari guided for revenues of €6.7 billion, EBITDA of €2.6 billion, and EBIT of €1.9 billion. They achieved those targets already in 2024, two years in advance.

However, even then, they weren't as conservative. Their guidance reflected a 9% revenue CAGR, about a 600 bps improvement in margins, and almost a doubling in EPS.

Coming into Capital Markets Day, the bogey was revenue guidance at mid-to-high single digits, and Ferrari came far below.

As I wrote many times in previous articles, it has gotten to the point that analysts and investors almost completely ignore the company's guidance. However, following the recent disappointing second quarter, investors are less confident in underwriting large beats like they have been previously.

Management Commentary Didn't Help

Ahead of the Capital Markets Day, investors and analysts, knowing how Ferrari works, braced for prudent guidance. The questions were a) How prudent, and b) What will be the explanation.

We already answered the first question; let's get to the second one, using quotes from Ferrari's CFO.

To preserve exclusivity per model through our 4 product pillars, volume growth has been designed to remain very limited throughout the plan period.

One of the biggest concerns on investors' minds was Ferrari's intentions regarding volume growth. Well, there's your answer. Very limited volume growth means 0%-1%, at best. This makes Ferrari's path to consistent growth very challenging.

As usual, the real challenge is to design a product portfolio evolution for a smooth and linear development of our profitability despite the natural cadence of model launches and phaseout.

This is something I discussed in my last article. When you don't increase volumes and generally refrain from raising prices on the same model, you're left with the task of perfectly timing phase-ins and phase-outs of models that will deliver steady average-selling-price growth.

Notably, in 2025, the phase-ins and phase-outs are leading to minimal ASP growth, and with volumes being flat, that's how we got to the weak second quarter that caused a major selloff.

There could be a simple way to grow revenues and margins. For example, expanding the numbers of Icona and Special Series would be something that would help immediately. But then our business model would be destroyed very rapidly.

One clear thing is that Ferrari's management prioritizes scarcity above all else. They seem to be highly cautious of the possibility that they will dilute Ferrari's brand value in any way.

As outsiders, it's easy for us to be greedy and want them to release the valve, but I think investors should trust the management that delivered such exceptional results.

Still, the bottom line is that management didn't give investors much room to be overly confident about a sizeable beat. The one thing they repeatedly mentioned is that they assumed lower personalization penetration given the higher ASPs, and that they left room for agility in terms of technology investments.

Valuation & No Near-Term Catalysts

The main reason for my downgrade isn't the underwhelming guidance. I'm quite confident Ferrari will exceed its plan. I am downgrading the stock for two reasons.

Limited Upside

At the current stock price, Ferrari trades at 41 times forward earnings based on the 2025 guidance, and at an exit multiple of 31 based on the 2030 guide.

This is, without question, a lofty multiple for a company that's growing at a single-digit rate, let alone below 5%.

Historically, I justified Ferrari's multiple by its unparalleled brand value and consistent double-digit growth. Even if you're very bullish on Ferrari's exceeding its 2030 plan, I don't think it's reasonable to assume it will more than double its projections.

Lack Of Near-Term Catalysts

It will take some time for investors to get over the Capital Markets letdown. The best cure will be Ferrari delivering strong results, with accelerating revenue growth.

However, all arrows point to a third quarter that's even worse than Q2, as they continue to phase out the high-priced Daytona, and the F80 replacement is set to begin shipments only in Q4.

Meanwhile, they already upgraded their guidance for 2025, so there's not much they can do to drive enthusiasm, at least not until Q4.

Conclusion

Ferrari is still one of my favorite companies, and I still view it as arguably the widest-moat company in the world.

However, I'm no longer as bullish as I was on its ability to deliver market-beating financial results. It started showing cracks in Q2, and now it dropped the hammer with an extremely disappointing 2030 guidance.

Although I'm confident Ferrari will exceed its targets, the lack of near-term catalysts and a still demanding valuation give me pause.

As such, I downgrade Ferrari to 'Hold'.
